entry.title: Where can I learn how to set-up SAP S/4HANA Sales and configure the essential functions?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Configuring SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Sales learning journey.

Overview 
In this Learning journey consultants and project team members will learn how to perform configuration tasks to adapt the standard functions and settings of the SAP system to the company's business needs.

Learning objectives
After completing this learning journey, application consultants will be able to set-up SAP S/4HANA Sales and configure the essential functions.

  • Setting up the Enterprise Structures in Sales
  • Configuring Business Partner and Customer Master Data in SAP S/4HANA Sales
  • Customizing of Sales Documents: Setting up of a Sales Document Type, Item Categories and Schedule Line Categories
  • Data Flow – Setting up the Copying Control in Sales
  • Partner Functions in Sales
  • Customizing output control and text control
  • Incomplete Documents (coming soon)
  • Outline Agreements (coming soon)

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area. 

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you. 

I appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Upgrade your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition following SAP Activate
entry.content:
We just launched the Roadmap Viewer for SAP Activate for RISE with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - Upgrade. In this blog post, I want to give you a quick rundown of what you can find there.

Let’s begin with the basics: What is SAP Activate?

SAP Activate is the methodology used to implement SAP solutions. It follows an Agile approach and simplifies project management by breaking it down into manageable tasks across various workstreams, such as Project Management, Solution Adoption, and Customer Team Enablement.

While many tasks are applicable to any project, some are specific to the solution, approach or project type. This blog post will focus on the specific activities to consider at each stage of an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition upgrade.

This graph gives a quick overview of the task I am talking about:

Let’s take a closer look by phase.

Discover Your Solution

The Discover phase is about what happens before you officially-kick off your SAP S/4HANA project. By the time you finish this phase, your organization should have an SAP S/4HANA upgrade plan, supported by a business case.

Here are some key tasks to focus on during this phase:

Let’s check them out a bit more closely:

Experience the latest SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

To really understand the potential benefits of the latest SAP S/4HANA version, it helps to have access to an actual SAP S/4HANA system. You can set up a 30-day trial system pretty quickly using the SAP Cloud Appliance Library. It’s hosted on Microsoft Azure, AWS, or Google Cloud, but keep in mind that hosting costs apply, and you’ll need an account with the cloud provider.

Upgrade Readiness Assessment

To plan your project, you must evaluate what it will take to run an SAP S/4HANA upgrade. The SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA is a super useful, automated tool that helps you assess your current SAP S/4HANA. It identifies simplification items that could affect your system, as well as analyzing custom code, add-ons, business functions, and impacted SAP Fiori apps.

This tool also tells you about compatibility features still in use, which are functionalities created for SAP ERP but available in SAP S/4HANA for a limited time. Just a heads up: usage rights for these features will expire soon, so it’s essential to replace them as part of your upgrade.

 

Prepare your Upgrade

The primary focus during this phase is organizing all the planning details. This is when your project officially begins. This includes defining your objectives and creating a detailed plan. Additionally, you assign members to the project team.

Here are some key tasks to focus on during this phase:

Let’s check some of them more thoroughly.

Transition Planning for Upgrade

  • Evaluate Impacts on SAP Fiori Apps and Other UIs: You should take a look at how SAP Fiori apps and other UIs will be affected in the current SAP S/4HANA system. For that, you’ll analyze which apps are outdated or no longer supported and find out what their replacements are. You'll also document your findings to determine what needs to be done in the upgraded SAP S/4HANA system. This means adding the new apps, getting rid of the old ones, reapplying any extensions, and planning how to help the business users who will be impacted by these changes.
  • Custom Code Analysis: As you are upgrading your SAP S/4HANA, it’s crucial to analyze the existing custom code and document the objects that will be impacted.
  • Choosing the Right Upgrade Approach: Based on your business needs, you should define the best way to upgrade your SAP S/4HANA system. When planning the upgrade, it's important to check out the different approaches and downtime optimization options right from the start since they can affect your timeline, the amount of work involved, and the cost.
  • Planning the upgrade Cycles: To guarantee a seamless upgrade process, it's important to create a well-structured plan. This includes determining the number of cycles you will go through. Ideally, you should aim for at least four cycles: sandbox, development, quality assurance, and final production. If you come across any problems or opt for a particular upgrade method, you may need to add extra cycles.
  • Considering Dual Maintenance: While you’re upgrading your SAP S/4HANA, there might be times when you need to make some changes to the production environment before everything is set in stone. This means you might have to set up a temporary system to handle those changes while the main system is being upgraded. Also, you’ll need to make sure any updates you make sync up with the upgraded systems. So, it’s a good idea to evaluate if you need a dual maintenance process.

Upgrade Preparation

  • Request Additional Temporary Systems:  Request any temporary systems you might need for the SAP S/4HANA upgrade project, keeping in line with the upgrade strategy. This should include things like a sandbox, which gives you a safe space to test changes before you touch the productive landscape. Having a sandbox helps the project team spot potential issues, determine what changes are needed, and plan ahead, which ultimately helps reduce downtime during the actual upgrade. If you ask for these systems early, you'll give the SAP Enterprise Cloud Services team enough time to set them up according to your project timeline.
  • Define Data Validation Tools: When upgrading to SAP S/4HANA, it's super important to make sure your business data remains consistent. You'll want to choose the right tools to keep everything on track and check that the data is in good shape both before and after the upgrade.

Fit to Standard Preparation for specific features

These workshops are a direct result of the upgrade Readiness Assessment, specifically the Conduct Functional Review Workshop during the Discover phase. It includes features that you are replacing, such as items in compatibility scope and new functionalities that you are not currently using but will implement during your upgrade.

The fit-to-standard analysis and design includes the following tasks:

During Prepare, you must focus on the first two:

  • Workshop Preparation: Get ready for the Fit-to-Standard workshops by making sure the relevant processes are set up in the initial system. Each workshop is co-led by an experienced SAP consultant and a business lead. Together, they will develop a workshop schedule detailing the content, agendas, presenters, participants, timings, and locations, ensuring clear boundaries and integration points between workshops.
  • Select the right system: It's super important to choose the right system for these activities. You’ll want to consider options like the SAP S/4HANA Fully-Activated Appliance, or a previously upgraded system (if you have one). Don’t forget to wrap up any pending tasks, like creating sample data, so everything is set for the workshops.

 

Explore the Solution

The Explore phase involves running the first upgrade of a sandbox system. After the technical upgrade, there are a few things to keep in mind, like getting SAP Fiori up and running.

Once the upgrade is done, the project team will do some initial testing to ensure that everything that shouldn’t have been affected remains fine. Additionally, you'll dive deep into the current custom code and review the integrations. This analysis isn’t just about listing what’ll be affected; it’s also about figuring out how and when to switch over to options that fit with clean core principles.

Here are some key tasks to focus on during this phase:

Let’s check some of them more thoroughly.

Upgrade Execution of the Sandbox System

You start by creating an SAP S/4HANA sandbox system from the existing production system and perform a full upgrade. You can't move on to the Realize phase until the sandbox upgrade is successful.

Here’s a quick rundown of the steps involved:

  1. Pre-Upgrade Activities: After cloning the production system, make sure the sandbox meets all technical requirements. Check compatibility with add-ons and tools, and update any necessary SAP Notes. Document everything for future reference, which means creating a Runbook. Before diving into the technical upgrade, tackle any SAP S/4HANA simplification items that need fixing. Also, run and log results from the business data validation reports.
  2. Upgrade: Ask the Enterprise Cloud Services team to run the SUM tool for upgrading your SAP S/4HANA. Yes, SAP will take care of SUM and contact you only if needed😊.
  3. Post-upgrade activities: Follow the “Upgrade Guide for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition” to complete all the technical tasks pending. Also, run the business data validation reports again and compare results with the source system. If there are discrepancies, investigate and ensure it’s ok to proceed.

Additional Activities in the upgraded Sandbox System

After you finish the technical conversion, there are a few steps to make sure everything's ready for the next phase:

  • SAP Fiori activities: After the upgrade, don’t forget to run the activities to ensure that SAP Fiori is working smoothly. Also, make sure to enable the new Fiori apps for testing.
  • Run Initial Testing: Validate that the upgrade didn’t impact any unexpected processes by running your usual end-to-end scenarios. Consider using the new SAP Fiori for some steps to see its value and rethink your user experience strategy.

Fit-to-Standard Workshops for Specific Features

As explained before the fit-to-standard analysis and design process helps to get approval for changes to the project scope and design before moving into the realization phase. These workshops come from the Conversion Readiness Assessment done in the Discover phase.

Just to refresh your mind, the fit-to-standard analysis and design includes the following tasks:

Here are the activities to be conducted during the Explore phase (from 3 to 6):

  • Fit-to-Standard Workshops & Documentation: These workshops help ensure the solution meets the business needs and processes set in the Prepare phase. Business process experts explore functionalities and figure out any additional requirements, which get documented in the project backlog. It's crucial to document all decisions and requirements from the workshops. Technical experts also document all the change requirements in detail.
  • Validating Requirements: Solution architects and project managers work together to ensure all requirements are accurately captured and classified. This helps prioritize which requirements to focus on and decide whether to keep old features or adopt new ones.
  • Design Workshops & documentation: Next, you run a design workshop to document configurations and custom development needed based on the delta requirements prioritized earlier. This is crucial for accurate estimates in the Realize phase.
  • Final Review and Sign-Off: The project architects review the design and make necessary adjustments, ensuring all parties agree on the requirements and proposed solutions. They document and categorize any issues and update the backlog for the Realize phase. Once they have the official approval and necessary documents, the project confidently moves to the next phase.

Custom Code Deep-Dive Analysis

When running an upgrade, an important part of the process is identifying the custom code that will be impacted. Also plan a move following the clean core approach. The main tasks here are:

  • Make a List of Custom Code Objects Affected by the Upgrade: Write down the custom objects you need to tweak if you want them to work after the upgrade. 
  • Make a List of Custom Code Objects That Can Move to Clean Core: Note down which custom objects can be shifted to the new extensibility options.
  • Set Up Coding Guidelines for New Custom Development Requests: Create some development rules so that any new code fits into the clean core approach.

Integration Review and Redesign

At this stage, you should list the integrations you currently have and evaluate which ones may be affected by the system upgrade. After that, create a strategy to address any potential issues or replace those integrations with new technology from SAP Solutions.

  • Analyze Current Integrations: Start by using SAP Readiness Check and the ABAP test cockpit to check all your inbound and outbound integrations. Then, figure out which interfaces are affected.
  • Define the Target Integration: Next, lay out a game plan for replacing outdated technologies with tools like SAP Integration Suite or SAP Process Orchestration. The idea is to move away from old methods like IDocs to more modern options like APIs or OData services to improve functionality and keep maintenance straightforward. While you're focusing on the integrations that will be impacted by the system upgrade, this is also a great chance to create a roadmap for upgrading all your integrations in the long run.
  • Apply Clean Core Principles: When it comes to integration, it's best to stick to standard SAP methods (like OData, SOAP, and events), and make sure you're orchestrating effectively between SAP S/4HANA and any external platforms. Security also needs to be up to date. Prioritize standard integrations and consider custom interfaces only when there’s no other choice.

 

Realize Your Requirements

In the Realize phase, you will upgrade the development and quality systems. You will also implement new requirements based on the design and tweak the impacted custom code as needed. You will set up the necessary integrations. Once everything is adjusted, the project team will jump into the planned testing.

Here are some key tasks to focus on during this phase:

Let’s review them more thoroughly:

Upgrade of the Development System

You must run your upgrade following the Runbook documented in the sandbox system. The steps are the ones you already know:

  1. Pre-Upgrade Activities of the Development System
  2. Upgrade of the Development System
  3. Post-Upgrade Activities of the Development System

Additional Activities in the Upgraded Development System

After completing the technical upgrade of the development system, there are several important tasks you need to address to prepare everything for testing. Here’s a breakdown:

  • SAP Fiori activities: This includes updating the business roles related to replaced apps to reference their successors, creating or reviewing roles to ensure new applications are accessible to end-users, and recreating any necessary user personalization for replaced applications in the new version. Review the extensions of deprecated and obsolete applications in the source version against their successors in the target version, and plan remediation options according to the clean core guidance previously defined.
  • Custom Code Remediation: Next up is fixing the custom code. You’ll start with a prioritized list of the affected objects that may need to be cleaned up or redesigned to fit into a “clean core” concept. This step involves making functional tweaks and optimizing performance, along with potentially refreshing the user experience.
  • Fixing Integrations: You’ll need to make necessary updates to the interfaces and replace any outdated integrations highlighted during the Integration Review phase. This means modifying some custom code, incorporating new standard integrations, and creating workarounds for custom scenarios if standard options aren’t available.
  • Solution Configuration: You’ll configure additional features in iterative cycles, focusing on the business scenarios and process requirements you outlined earlier. This will normally occur in two-week sprints, during which you’ll tackle system setup, documentation, and unit testing according to the agreed-upon backlog. Every change will be documented, and you'll collect all customizing transports along the way.

Upgrade of the Quality System

Before you jump into integration testing, you must upgrade the quality assurance system. Ideally QAS should be a fresh copy of the productive. This is especially important if this will be your last cycle before moving to production.

Going back to the upgrade, you already know how it works, so just make sure to handle all the functional and technical tasks based on the Runbook.

Cutover Preparation

When you're getting ready to upgrade the production system, it's super important to have a solid cutover plan in place. This plan covers all the steps you need to take, from the initial prep work to the final upgrade. Usually, there’s a cutover manager who makes sure everything stays on schedule.

The cutover plan won't dive into all the technical details like a runbook would, but it's a good idea to pull out the key tasks from the runbook to keep everything on track. Don’t forget to include a backup plan just in case you need to roll back the upgrade for any reason.

 

Deploy & Run Your Solution

In this part of the project, you'll do the final upgrade and get the system up and running. Just a heads up, you might have to cut back on some sleep while you're handling Hypercare Support.

Here are some key tasks to focus on during these phases:

Let’s check them out more closely.

Upgrade of the Production System (Cutover)

To wrap up the production system upgrade, you need to tackle all the tasks in the cutover plan that you’ve worked through in the earlier cycles. Just make sure to follow the detailed steps in the runbook.

System Go-Live and Hypercare Support

The system's go-live is the last big step in a successful SAP S/4HANA upgrade. At this point, everything is up and running in the production environment, and then you move into the hypercare phase to help everyone get used to the upgraded system.

Hypercare is the period right after go-live where you're here to tackle any questions or issues that pop up. You’ll be doing daily checks to keep a close eye on how well the new solution is being adopted. It’s super important to see how the system is performing and make any tweaks if needed.

Continuous Improvement and Innovation

You need to set up some IT maintenance processes to continuously improve the solutions. This means regularly rolling out feature packs and support packs to get the latest updates from SAP. It’s important for both the business and IT teams to work together on a planning cycle to decide which new features to implement.

Those improvements include deploying the UX strategy defined during the Prepare Phase and replacing integrations and custom code following the Clean Core approach defined during Explore.

We've come to the end of this blog post.  Just a reminder, this was a quick overview of some of the tasks you can find in the SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer for SAP Activate for RISE with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - Upgrade. Check out the details in the Roadmap Viewer to help you plan your upgrade project.

If you want to know more about upgrades, check out these documents:

  • Upgrading SAP S/4HANA—How, Why, and Best Practices
  • Embrace Agility and upgrades for success

I hope you found this blog post helpful. If you did, please give it a "like" or share it. I’d love to hear your thoughts and feedback, so feel free to leave a comment. If you have any questions, just drop them in the Q&A section with the tags SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. We'll try to get back to you as soon as we can.


Thanks,

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG team.

entry.title: Basic Overview on SAP S/4 Hana Cloud Private Edition on Azure
entry.content:
Hi 

Nowadays SAP S/4 Hana Cloud Private Edition is one the deployment option. It is being most demanding. Here I am going to share an Overview on SAP S/4 Hana Cloud Private Edition on Azure. It will be help to keep conceptual knowledge for SAP S/4 Hana Cloud Private Edition on Azure.

Introduction to SAP on Azure

SAP applications form the basis of many enterprise operations, including those of multinational companies. The enterprise resource planning (ERP) applications that SAP provides are highly complex and capable of providing the scalability and analytics that are necessary for many organizations. As organizations move to the cloud it is key that they can take these applications with them which is why Microsoft has partnered with SAP on Azure.

Usage of SAP on Azure

Combining your SAP and Azure operations can provide several benefits that are difficult to obtain with SAP or Azure alone. These include greater flexibility of operations, scalability, and integration of solutions.

Azure Is Hybrid- Azure offers support for hybrid operations beyond connectivity and infrastructure. It enables you to consistently perform operations regardless of platform, including identity management, application development, and security. Native and third-party integrations support these capabilities.

Azure Can Use Bare Metal for HANA-You can deploy HANA to VMs or bare metal servers in Azure. This enables you to ensure that resources are maintained in isolation with dedicated hardware. These servers come built-in with infrastructure for storage, compute, and networking. Additionally, bare metal servers are certified for HANA Tailored Data Center Integration.

Easy Integration of SAP and Microsoft Products-A long term partnership between Microsoft and SAP provides significant support for integrated solutions. For example, both Power BI and Office 365 support the use of SAP HANA. Additionally, both vendors use open standards and interfaces for data exchange, including OData. This enables you to integrate products smoothly with no need for data transformation.

Almost 100% Availability-Azure enables you to spread operations and data redundancies across AZs. Data is automatically replicated for durability and availability. You can also create additional backups or select geo-redundant storage for disaster recovery and failover protection.

Introduction to SAP S/4 Han a Cloud Private Edition and its features

SAP S/4 HANA  Cloud Private Edition is a tailored-to-fit cloud ERP that adapts to your organization's unique transformation. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition safeguards your existing investment in SAP ERP while benefiting from the latest capabilities and greater flexibility.

Running on your standard

  • Maximize your differentiation by tailoring business processes to your unique requirements​​
  • Protect your investment and move to the cloud at your pace

Modernizing your business

  • Achieve bottom-line growth with proven business processes for your industry
  • Deliver green-line growth by building sustainability directly into your business

Keeping control

  • Manage the speed of change by rolling out innovations on your timeline
  • Choose the cloud infrastructure based on your landscape and preferences

SAP S/4 Hana Cloud Private Edition on Azure

The Microsoft Cloud helps you unleash the power of your SAP applications and data. Whether you migrate to SAP S/4HANA on Azure, leverage RISE with SAP for a fully managed service, or add intelligence with Azure data and AI services, Microsoft provides a reliable, secure and comprehensive cloud platform to transform your SAP estate.

SAP solutions on Azure

Run SAP solutions across development, testing, and production scenarios in Azure. SAP on Azure solutions help you optimize your enterprise resource planning (ERP) in the cloud using the security features, reliability, and scalable SAP-certified infrastructure of Azure. This intelligent ERP suite is designed specifically for in-memory computing. Whether you want to run SAP S/4HANA in a new implementation or migrate from SAP Business Suite to SAP S/4HANA, Azure has you covered.

SAP S/4HANA on Azure benefits:

  • Seamless connectivity for end users who need to access SAP Fiori-based applications when using Azure ExpressRoute.
  • A 99.99 percent SLA for your critical ERP instances by running all product instances of SAP S/4HANA in a high-availability pair by running in two Azure Availability Zones.
  • Backup for SAP S/4HANA and SAP BW/4 HANA in seconds, with a low recovery-time objective—even for multi-terabyte databases.

Certification that SAP S/4HANA runs on SUSE Linux Enterprise and Red Hat Enterprise Linux servers. Plus, SAP HANA on Azure Virtual Machines  and SAP HANA on Azure large-instance machines are certified to run SAP S/4HANA. Microsoft Azure is one of the cloud infrastructure options for the RISE with SAP offering. On Azure, RISE with SAP leverages Microsoft’s global network infrastructure to provide connectivity to SAP applications in a secure and reliable way.

SAP S/4HANA on Azure offers:

• SAP on Azure VM workloads
• Azure Center for SAP solutions
• SAP on Azure deployment automation framework
• Azure Monitor for SAP solutions

There are multiple Microsoft Azure offerings for running and managing your SAP systems. These offerings range from traditional Azure virtual machine (VM) offerings to top-level Azure services, to tools that integrate with other Azure services or external products.

SAP on Azure VM workloads

You can run SAP workloads on the Azure platform using different Azure Virtual Machines (Azure VMs) offerings. Azure is certified for multiple SAP products, including SAP HANA and SAP NetWeaver products.

SAP Integration with Microsoft Services-In addition to the capabilities to run SAP IaaS and SaaS workloads on Azure, Microsoft offers various capabilities, scenarios, best-practice guides, and tutorials to integrate SAP workloads running anywhere with other Microsoft products and services. Among them are popular services such as Microsoft Entra ID, Exchange Online, Power Platform and Power BI, Azure Integration Services, Excel, SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Data Warehouse Cloud, and SAP Success Factors to name a few.

SAP HANA on Azure (Large Instances)-SAP HANA on Azure (Large Instances) is a solution that provides VMs for deploying and running SAP HANA.

Azure Center for SAP solutions

Azure Center for SAP solutions is a service that makes SAP a top-level workload in Azure. This end-to-end solution allows you to create and run SAP systems as a unified workload on Azure. You can use this service through the Azure portal, a REST API, and the Azure CLI.

SAP on Azure deployment automation framework

The SAP on Azure deployment automation framework is an open-source orchestration tool for deploying, installing, and maintaining SAP environments.

Azure Monitor for SAP solutions

Azure Monitor for SAP solutions is an Azure-native monitoring product for SAP landscapes that run on Azure, which uses specific parts of the Azure Monitor infrastructure.

Conclusion - It is secured and SAP Private Link serves as a great 'Private' connectivity option for certain use-cases enabling access to private service endpoints and avoids public endpoints, thereby resulting in transfer of data over private networks.

Reference-  www.SAP.com,

https://learn.microsoft.com/en-in/azure/sap/workloads/hana-vm-operations

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/quot-sap-private-link-and-azure-private-link-quot-in-the-context-of-sap/ba-p/13719685

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/rise-with-sap-s-4hana-cloud-private-edition-secure-cloud-connectivity/ba-p/13558064

entry.title: One Tool to Track Them All
entry.content:
We launched our process-tracking tool, Process Observer, over a decade ago. Since then, a lot has changed. Now, our goal is Cloud First, and we want our customers to RISE to the cloud and GROW in the cloud.

SAP acquired and integrated Signavio a while ago and our biggest question was - what is the future of Process Observer in the cloud and how does it play together with SAP Signavio Process Intelligence? The answer — A new kid on the block, "BUSINESS EVENT LOGGING (BEL)."

BEL uses a much leaner and more performant approach compared to Process Observer. It also requires less configuration on your part while still giving you the freedom to enhance and integrate your processes. At the same time, it allows you to easily integrate with SAP Signavio Process Intelligence.

 

 Fig. 1: Event Log Viewer App in Business Event Logging

 

Overview and Benefits

So, what is Business Event Logging?

  •  It collects the event data locally and you can use integration to extract the data into SAP Signavio Process Intelligence.
  • It is based on Business Events, but it is also planned to support other sources like Change Documents.
  • It provides field change information.
  • It is available now in SAP S/4HANA CE, SAP S/4HANA OP/PC and SAP ABAP Platform Cloud.

How can BEL be useful for you?

  • You can activate logging of events just by selecting the relevant Object Types. You can create process views (“cases”) in SAP Signavio Process Intelligence and analyze your processes there.
  • You can use the data viewer apps to review logged data in multiple aspects.
  • You can manage the data retention in the business event log with SAP ILM.
  • You can extend processes or log your own processes by creating your own Business Events.

 

The Foundation

Business events are object-centric events created in the RAP framework. They are assigned with an event binding and are C2-released by SAP. You can find these events documented in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, Follow the path: Explore / <your product> / Events.  

 

Fig. 2: Business Events in the BEL Accelerator Hub

The RAP framework is an extensible framework: you can create your own objects, create events with binding, extend SAP delivered objects with your events, or just extend an SAP-delivered event.

Business events can also contain change information. For more information, you can explore event definitions in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This change information is now visible in the BEL log.

If you require additional standard events delivered by SAP, or change information, you can raise a request via the Customer Influence Portal. .

Beyond these Business Events, there are internal events such as RAP events, SAP-delivered C1- events, and Change Documents for other BEL data sources. We plan to support them in the future and will keep you updated.

 

Release support

You might be wondering when you could start using this. The great news is that if you’re in the cloud you can start right away!

SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud & SAP BTP, ABAP Environment:

For the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408 or later and for SAP BTP, ABAP Environment 2411 or later, we support:

  • Logging of all released business events for all object types as listed in the SAP Accelerator Hub for the product.
  • Logging of customer events. For more information, see the instructions in the product documentation.

You can find the detailed documentation for Business Event Logging for these products here:

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (latest): User documentation  

SAP BTP, ABAP Environment (latest): User documentation  

SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud  & On-Premise:

The support in the SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition and On-Premise Edition depends on the release.

Functionalities provided with PC/OP 2021 FPS2: 

  • Only logging of Order-to-Cash related events provided by SAP (based on Business Event Handling). 
  • Customer extensibility is not supported by Business Event Handling.

Functionalities provided with PC/OP 2022 FPS1: 

  • Only logging of Order-to-Cash related events provided by SAP (based on RAP)  
  • Customer events are supported with FPS 2 of OP 2022

Functionalities provided with PC/OP 2023 FPS2: 

  • Logging of all Business Events provided by SAP is supported (as documented in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub).

Functionalities provided with S/4HANA Private Cloud / On Premise 2023 (latest):

  • You can view the list of functionalities in the User documentation.

We will cover the integration of the component and the customer extensibility in separate articles.

entry.title: Which S/4HANA extensibility option is the best for thriving as an SAP partner in the cloud space?
entry.content:
Get more value from S/4HANA with SAP Extensibility options, part 3

In my first article   of this series, I explained why extensibility options are crucial for S/4HANA customers. In the second article , I explained which option I should choose for the different use cases from a customer's point of view. In this last article, I want to focus on existing and new SAP partners who wish to connect their cloud offerings to S/4HANA customers or migrate their existing SAP ECC add-on to ABAP Cloud.

I have worked for almost 30 years in the SAP ERP space and have seen many innovations in SAP software. I started in 1996 when SAP introduced the client-server ERP and RFC libraries. During the turn of the century, SAP adopted the Internet and launched its new dimension portfolio, and third-party integration products were introduced into the market. SAP has stepped into the cloud in the last decade and fully adopted artificial intelligence.

There are a lot of changes at the core of ERP, but the challenges in my job are almost the same. As a developer, enterprise architect, SAP mentor, speaker, teacher, and advisor, I ask myself, ‘How can I add additional value for my customers out of an ERP system?’ And the answer is always the same. Focus on the missing functionality. SAP always delivers best-practice solutions that never fulfill all customers' requirements. This is still an ample opportunity for me, consultancy companies, SAP add-on solution providers, and all other software vendors.

For the last eight years, I have mostly worked for big cloud companies like Blackline, Booking, and Zuora to advise them to connect their cloud portfolio and systems to SAP S/4HANA. The cloud companies' customers are also big SAP customers. In this article, I will share my experience and tell you which S/4HANA extensibility options fit the best cloud vendors that want to thrive as SAP partners.

Challenges for SAP Solution Partners

The question is easy, but the answer is more complex. As I wrote in my previous article, SAP supports several S/4HANA extensibility options, which depend on the S/4HANA releases and versions. So you need to answer the question: Do you want to support the S/4HANA Cloud private version, the S/4HANA Cloud public version, or even support ‘classic’ SAP ECC?  But also, do you want to build a standalone add-on that runs on Business Technology Platform Software as a Service (BTP SAAS) or is embedded on S/4HANA? Or do you want to connect your partner cloud solution to S/4HANA?

But there are more answers needed. The data and events must be publicly available in the S/4HANA system. How will we handle the data exchange's latency, performance, size, and security? Who will be responsible when something goes wrong, technical, infra, and business-wise? Will the current SAP license be enough, or will there be additional operational and license costs for my customers? How will user management, authentication, authorization, and security be handled? How will the execution be monitored, and how will alerting and mapping be arranged? How should sensitive data be managed, and what audit trails and data should be archived? How will networking, security, risk, and complaint departments react to the solution? Will the used platform and tooling be future-proof? And so we can ask a lot more questions.

Ultimately, all these questions should be answered to develop the best solution. However, in my experience, not all SAP partners consider these questions.

SAP positions the S/4HANA Cloud public edition as the preferred version to GROW with SAP in the long term. However, SAP also provides the S/4HANA Cloud private edition for existing customers to RISE, with SAP as the golden path to move to the S/4HANA Cloud public edition in the future.

We will first focus on the S/4HANA Cloud public edition because this release is SAP’s long-term solution. Next, we can consider other SAP ERP systems, such as the S/4HANA Cloud private edition, S/4HANA on-premise, and SAP ECC.

S/4HANA extensibility options

In my second article, I described the eight extensibility options for S/4HANA. However, if we look at partner solutions, we can skip the classic extensibilities because they are not supported in the public edition and are obsolete from a certification point of view. Also, the low-code extensibilities options S/4HANA Key User Extensibility, SAP Analytics Cloud, and SAP Build Apps could be more suitable because they mainly focus on custom developments for the citizen developer.

Figure 1 – Possible Partner Integration Options

 

This limited the possible extensibility options, and because we can also access the S/4HANA APIs and events directly, we will have five possibilities (See Figure 1) to deliver a partner solution:

  • An add-on application on the S/4HANA embedded ABAP environment
  • A partner application as a SAAS application running on SAP BTP ABAP environment
  • A partner application as a SAAS application developed with SAP Build Code on SAP BTP
  • An integration package on SAP Cloud Integration
  • Running your application on a non-SAP solution and directly accessing S/4HANA events and APIs.

Using APIs and events

Software vendors that run their solutions on their cloud will primarily consider the last option and try to connect their cloud solution directly to S/4HANA events and APIs. But from my experience, this isn’t the right approach. The S/4HANA Cloud public edition strongly depends on business events, and most cloud vendor solutions aren’t prepared for this. Due to the configuration possibilities in the S/4HANA Cloud and the use of APIs, the responsibility for root cause analysis shifts from the customer to the cloud vendor, which needs to build a big SAP knowledge support center. Also, the cloud vendor should handle all security, risk, and compliance issues, which are initially the customer's responsibility.

Another issue with this approach is that you, as a partner, cannot certify the solution with SAP. SAP is not responsible for the solution's availability and performance, code changes, or deprecation of APIs and events. This approach carries a high risk for customers and the cloud vendor.

SAP Cloud Integration

A better approach is an integration package that can be installed on the customer's SAP Cloud Integration system. In this case, SAP will ensure smooth integration between S/4HANA and SAP Cloud Integration. The solution can be certified, and as a cloud vendor, you can mainly focus on integrating your cloud partner solution and SAP Cloud integration. The downside of this approach is that SAP Cloud Integration is separate from any S/4HANA Cloud edition license and should be brought by the customer separately. Only some customers are willing to do so. Most customers already have an existing integration product for more than just their SAP landscape. The need for separate SAP Cloud Integration licenses and setting up a support and knowledge center to maintain SAP Cloud Integration will be a blocker. Providing an integration package as a partner solution will limit the number of SAP customers you can connect to your cloud solution in the market.

ABAP Cloud environments

The best approach for cloud vendors is to build the connectivity as an add-on product on S/4HANA, a SAAS solution on BTP, or a combination of these options. Building your partner integration solution on an ABAP Cloud environment is the best extensibility option for applications strongly dependent on S/4HANA. Based on my investigation, cloud vendors will profit the most from this option. They can shift the responsibility of the availability and maintenance of the landscape to SAP; they are highly flexible in a robust and stable environment following SAP’s future path and can directly profit from the innovations SAP is launching. The barriers to developing these extensions are higher, but the barriers for customers will be lower. There will be no additional costs for running the developer extensions on the embedded ABAP cloud environment in S/4HANA Cloud. When cloud vendors run their solution as a SAAS solution on BTP, they can share the costs with multiple customers. The advantage of the SAAS solution above the Addon is that the cloud vendor fully controls the code line, and the customer is still responsible for accessing and connecting to their S/4HANA tenant.

Another significant advantage of building connectivity from S/4HANA to your cloud solution on ABAP Cloud for BTP as a SAAS solution is that you can connect to the S/4HANA cloud public edition, make your future-proof solution with one code line, and downport the connection to other ERP versions, such as the S/4HANA cloud private edition, S/4HANA on-premise, or even SAP ECC.

Figure 2 – Codeline options of ABAP Cloud

 

Because the ABAP Cloud on the BTP code line follows SAP’s Clean core strategy, it should also run as an add-on on the S/4HANA Cloud editions when these editions support the same underlying ABAP Cloud version.

As mentioned before, in SAP ECC, many SAP solution partners have already provided an add-on to solve the missing functionality of standard SAP. However, this add-on should be rewritten to be S/4HANA Cloud compliant because SAP only accepts code that follows SAP’s Clean core strategy. The SAP solution partner can rewrite the code as S/4HANA Cloud Developer Extensibility, but in this case, the partner always depends on the customers' upgrade strategy. Suppose he wants to control the versioning of his code line and innovations. In that case, the partner can rewrite the addon as a side-by-side extensibility with SAP Build Code in SAP CAP or ABAP, which connects to S/4HANA. Or he can even run the applications in a non-SAP cloud environment of his choice and follow the extensibility options described above. From my experience, all these options are valid, future-proof, and in line with the solution for cloud vendors.

Cloud and SAP Add-on vendors who want to build their solutions with ABAP Cloud can even speed up their development, shorten the time to market, and shift responsibilities back to their customers using an OEM solution like the FEXS API Connect from fexs.com. These OEM solutions already take care of the most common tasks, resulting in less development needed, smaller SAP support departments, and higher customer satisfaction.

Conclusion

Because SAP and its customers are still on the move with S/4HANA to its future Cloud ERP landscape, it isn’t easy for SAP Solution partners to make the right decision for their future-proof solution.  The move of SAP to the cloud and the openness and flexibility of its portfolio will give SAP Addon partners and existing cloud solution providers an excellent opportunity to bring added value to the existing SAP community.  Suppose your solution strongly depends on S/4HANA processes. In that case, I believe a partner application running as S/4HANA Cloud Developer Extensibility or on a SAP BTP ABAP Environment following SAP Clean Core strategy is the best and most future-proof solution. An article is always too short to explain all the details, but I hope this article triggers your mind.  And in my job as a strategic architect and advisor, I’m open to any discussion and available to help push you in the right direction.

This article was initially posted on the linked-in profile of Robert Eijpe

entry.title: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – Updated Transport to Production Strategy for Rapid Activation
entry.content:
If you are operating with SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA, you will need to transport your launchpad content, layout, and other configuration settings from your Development to your Production environment. You can now find an updated document with the latest advice on the transport strategy for SAP Fiori Rapid Activation and related SAP Fiori objects at

SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - Rapid Activation Transport Move to Production Strategy

 

What is SAP Fiori Rapid Activation?

As a reminder, the SAP Fiori Rapid Activation procedure lets you, as a system administrator, quickly activate the capabilities of the SAP Fiori launchpad and SAP Fiori applications using a series of task lists.

Refer to

  • As explained in the central note for all SAP S/4HANA releases, SAP Note 2902673 - Rapid Activation for SAP Fiori in SAP S/4HANA - Overview
  • SAP Help - SAP Fiori Overview - Rapid Activation of SAP Fiori
  • SAP Blog - SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – Activate SAP Fiori in 1 day or less - the video playlist and FAQ
  • SAP Blog - SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – Creating your custom business roles – the end-to-end process and video playlist

What’s changed in this version?

This document has the latest information for SAP S/4HANA 2023 release including

  • Considerations for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition customers
  • Clearer explanation of how transport of Rapid Activation works
  • Prerequisites for transporting Rapid Activation
  • Which task lists to run on implementation versus upgrade
  • Mandatory/Optional tasks to run in Development versus subsequent systems
  • Critical parameters for transports
  • Coverage of new task list SAP_FIORI_FCM_CATALOG_ACTIVATION
  • How to transport settings in other Rapid Activation tools such as Launchpad configuration parameters in transaction /UI2/FLP_CUS_CONF

For official guidance for your SAP S/4HANA release and FPS, always refer to the Composite SAP note: Rapid Activation for SAP Fiori in SAP S/4HANA <release> <FPSxx>" for your SAP S/4HANA release and FPS (example: SAP Note 3469488 - Composite SAP note: Rapid Activation for SAP Fiori in SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS02)

The links to these SAP notes are available in the document.

Becoming a SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA guru


You’ll find much more on the community topic page for SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Other helpful links in the SAP Community:

  • See all questions and answers about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 
  • Follow SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA for more blogs and updates 
  • Ask a Question about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA


Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG

entry.title: In-House Repair 2.0, the in-house repair for the private cloud
entry.content:
When we started in 2018 with the new In-House Repair for SAP S/4HANA, we have been focusing on the public cloud with the option to downport the innovations to the private cloud. In the meantime, the codelines between public and private cloud have been splitted. We have taken this as an advantage to focus the development consequently on the needs of customers relying on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. 

In-House Repair in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition 

In October 2024 we have release the In-House Repair for the private cloud. Internally we call it In-House Repair 2.0 since it is a major update with huge benefits for manufacturers and service providers. The design is ready for supporting new service models, it is highly integrated in the SAP S/4HANA and the SAP Service Management solutions landscape. This is valid for the whole service management solution in private cloud. 

The major change in In-House Repair itself is the ability to adjust to the customers' individual requirements while offering comprehensive and transparent processes for the in-house service business.

The main characteristics:  

1. Combines In-House Repair with Field Service

Many companies are supporting their customers onsite with field services and, at the same time, are running in-house service centers for service and repair. The new in-house repair uses the integrated service management capabilities of S/4HANA Service and allows to combine field services with in-house repair in the same service order.

In the example, a service manager creates a service quotation for a field service, the dismantling of a component on-site and an in-house diagnosis by an expert. The repair object defines the component to be sent to the in-house service center for diagnosis and repair. The repair object integrates with customer returns functionality to manage logistics. Once the quote is accepted, a service order is created and ready to be performed. 

2. Provides strong logistics capabilities

The main differentiator of in-house repair are the logistics capabilities. From a mobile speed control camera up to a hundred tons heavy turbine, the repair objects have to be transported from the customer to the in-house service center.  

In the demo case, the inbound logistics is triggered in a one-click action. A returns order is created in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition to manage the transport to the service center.

3. Extends quotation capabilities

At any point in time of the in-house repair process, there may be the need to communicate additional work to the customer. The new In-House Repair supports the requote functionality. It allows to create as many service quotations as needed and updates the existing service order with the changes. 

In the demo, the service managers creates a requote based on the diagnosis results. On accept of the customer, the existing service order is updated. 

4. Offers ready-to-use apps for the daily business

Usability is an important aspect. User-friendly apps support the service managers and service technicians in their business. The new app "Manage Repair Objects" allows service managers and service technicians to concentrate on their business. 

In the example, the service manager filters for those repair objects which have been received by the service center and where the service order is ready to be executed. 

5. Brings transparency from quotation to execution until billing and logistics

A requirement which has been stated by all customers from the very beginning is transparency. Service is teamwork. An E2E service requires logistics, planning, execution and billing. It is a challenge to keep the overview and push the repair for a short execution time.

The demo shows the graphical transaction history of the new in-house repair. It summarizes all service transaction and the user can easily navigate to each document.

6. Analyzes Profitability and Margin

In-House Repair is an integral part of the S/4HANA Service. The service management application allows ad-hoc profitability analysis including revenue recognition. It makes use of the item based accounting capabilities of SAP S/4HANA where cost and revenue is available immediately for profit and margin analysis.

The demo shows the app "Product and Service Margins" with its drill-down capabilities.

7. Comes with Best Practices Content 7GB 

The new In-House Repair is delivered with a new scope item for best practice content. Project teams can easily setup an initial system by using pre-defined customising settings and walk through the process with comprehensive test scripts. 

Search for scope item 7GB in the processnavigator of your SAP for Me portal. A login is required to get access to the scope item content. 

What comes next?

We are continuously enhancing the in-house repair in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition. Please check out the In-House Repair roadmap on SAP Roadmap-Explorer.

entry.title: How to make the "billing date" field editable in billing plan view of the service contract
entry.content:
How to make the "billing date" field editable in billing plan view of the service contract

Welcome to this blog, in which we will show you how you can tune your Service Contract Billing Plan so that you are able to edit the billing date in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition.

Introduction

In the world of service contracts, flexibility is key to managing customer relationships effectively. However, certain aspects of billing plans are locked by default, causing sometimes unnecessary rigidity in a dynamic business environment.

One such aspect is the billing date in the billing plan, which, in its original state, is not editable. This limitation can be a bottleneck for businesses that need to adjust billing dates based on customer requests or specific business needs. You can see below a screenshot with editable Billing Date Column.

Figure 1 Example of 'Billing Date' column with editable fields

But here's a way to unlock this field: with a little bit of technical tweaking, you can make the billing date field editable. By leveraging the BAdI (Business Add-In) CRM_BILLREQ_I_FC, you can gain control over the editability of fields in billing request lines, providing the flexibility your business requires.

What is BAdI CRM_BILLREQ_I_FC?

BAdI CRM_BILLREQ_I_FC is part of an enhancement spot CRM_BILLREQ_I that allows you to control the editability of fields in billing request lines. By implementing this BAdI, you can specify which fields should be editable and which should remain read-only, tailored to your specific business requirements.

 

Where to find the BAdI CRM_BILLREQ_I_FC

You need to navigate through the following path where you can find this BAdI in the SAP system:

SPRO -> Service -> Transactions -> Basic Settings -> Billing Plan -> Business Add-Ins -> BAdI: Field Checks for Billing Request Lines

Or simply start transaction SE18 and enter the BAdI Name.

Figure 2 BAdI Builder Transaction SE18

 

Understanding this BAdI

The BAdI provides a method called FIELDCHECK that is particularly useful. This method takes two important importing parameters:

  • IS_FIELDCHECK_COM:      A communication structure containing information about the billing request, including its GUID and items.
  • IV_EXTERNAL_CALL:          An indicator for external calls.

The implementation can modify the changing parameter ‘CT_INPUT_FIELD_NAMES’ table to specify which fields should be editable or read-only. Each field in the table is assigned a value in the `changeable` field:

  • `' '`: Editable
  • `'A'`: Read-only

 

Steps to Implement the BAdI

1. Access the BAdI Implementation:

Using the SPRO path mentioned or “SE18”, navigate to the BAdI implementation screen. Search for the BAdI definition “CRM_BILLREQ_I” and select it.

2. Create BAdI Implementation:

Create a new BAdI implementation or copy and enhance an existing one.

3. Enhance the FIELDCHECK Method:

Within the BAdI implementation, navigate to the method `FIELDCHECK`. Inside the FIELDCHECK method, add logic to set the `changeable` field to a space (' ') for the billing date field, making it editable.

Adjust the `CT_INPUT_FIELD_NAMES` table to specify the editability of the fields based on the business logic and needs.

Here is a simple ABAP code snippet example:

METHOD IF <business_condition_satisfied>

READ TABLE ct_input_field_names INTO <fs_input_field>                        WITH KEY fieldname = 'BILL_DATE'.

IF sy-subrc IS INITIAL. <fs_input_field>-changeable = ' '. " Set field to editable

ENDIF.

ENDLOOP.

ENDMETHOD.

```

4. Activate the BAdI:

Ensure that your BAdI implementation is active by saving and activating the changes in transaction `SE18`.

Additional Tips

  • Refer to BAdI Documentation:
    • For more detailed information and specific use cases, refer to the BAdI documentation available within the SAP system.
  • Use CRM_ORDER_READ or Billing API:          
    • You can use `CRM_ORDER_READ` or other billing APIs to retrieve relevant details. This might involve updating internal tables to make the field editable as required.

By following these steps, you can achieve a more flexible billing plan that aligns with your business needs, allowing you to better serve your customers.

Conclusion

Unlocking the ability to edit the billing date in the billing plan is not just a technical achievement; it's an enhancement that can improve your user experience and customer satisfaction. With the power of BAdI CRM_BILLREQ_I_FC, you can tailor your billing process to be as responsive as your business requires.

 

Disclaimer:

The customer and business individual implementation of this BAdI is on your own responsibility and liability. Please validate any possible impact on related business processes (e.g. billing and settlement) and follow up processes such as financial revenue recognition, controlling thoroughly.

If you are considering implementing a BAdI or any custom development in an SAP system, it is always advisable to:

- Conduct thorough testing in a development or sandbox environment.

- Validate the changes against standard SAP procedures and best practices.

- Ensure that any custom development is documented and reviewed by experts.

- Follow SAP guidelines for version control and change management.

entry.title: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – [Updated] Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori
entry.content:
As you may already know, SAP recommends deploying SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA with your SAP Fiori Front-End Server in embedded mode (embedded FES) , as outlined in the SAP Fiori Deployment Options and System Landscape Recommendations. 

Embedded FES reduces server cost and complexity and simplifies ongoing operations as you grow your usage of SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA innovations.

If you began your SAP S/4HANA journey with a SAP Fiori Front-End Server in standalone mode (standalone FES), you might be asking:
How do I transition from standalone FES to embedded FES deployment?

The Regional Implementation Group for SAP S/4HANA has worked with many customers to help them move to SAP Fiori Front-End Server Embedded deployment. Based on these experiences, we have created the first version of the document in 2019 as an easy-to-follow, step-by-step guide. This guide outlined the migration process, explaining both the technical setup and how to ensure a smooth experience for users.

We’re excited to announce our updated Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA guide based on SAP S/4HANA release 2023.

This new version provides more streamlined steps, updated tools and further recommendations to help you successfully move from standalone FES to embedded FES deployment.

Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Why do you need this guide?

This guide is essential for SAP S/4HANA customers who are looking to move from a standalone FES to an embedded FES deployment. It provides clear, step-by-step instructions to make the transition smooth, covering both technical and practical aspects of the migration.

Whether you are upgrading your SAP S/4HANA system or aligning with SAP's latest architectural recommendations, this guide ensures you have everything you need i.e. from planning, installation, and configuration to post-migration testing and optimization. It helps you:

  • Understand the benefits of moving to embedded FES deployment.
  • Know the right time to make the shift.
  • Navigate technical prerequisites, sizing, and system considerations.
  • Simplify the migration process with actionable steps, tools, and examples.
  • Ensure a seamless experience for users by resolving common issues.
  • Minimize disruptions by retaining user personalization and role assignments.
  • Gain tips on how to enhance usability and performance

By following this guide, you can align with SAP best practices to unlock the full potential of SAP Fiori embedded FES deployment.

 

What's in it / What's new?

  • Enhance performance with tips for optimizing launchpad features, OData services, and app configurations

    • This includes reviewing and improving performance for SAP GUI Apps, SAP Fiori Apps, SAP Fiori Launchpad etc. and best practices to adjust and transport launchpad configuration parameters
  • Why and how to use Clean Core SAP Fiori administration tools to perform the migration
    • Clean core tools like Launchpad Content Manager, Launchpad Application Manager, Manage Launchpad Pages, Manage Launchpad Spaces provide enhanced capabilities compared to classical tools like Launchpad Designer. 
  • Structured 6-Step Content Migration
    • Comprehensive steps to migrate roles, catalogs, spaces and pages.
  • User Personalization Retention
    • Tools like /UIF/MIGRATE_FES_PERSO to migrate homepages, private views, and UI changes.
  • Multi-Client Activation
    • Detailed guidance on enabling and configuring new clients post-migration.
  • Improved Testing and Troubleshooting
    • Expanded scenarios for UAT, error handling using App Support, and validation tools.
  • Performance and usability enhancements
    • Parameters for faster navigation, optimized search, and stateful SAP GUI containers.
  • Web Dispatcher Guidance
    • Steps to switch from Standalone to Embedded without changing end-user URLs.

 

Becoming a SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA guru

You’ll find much more on our  SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA topic page

Other helpful links:

  • See all questions and answers about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 
  • Follow SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA for more blogs and updates 
  • Ask a Question about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG and Customer Care team.

entry.title: The Trace – Watching Object Dependencies at Work
entry.content:
Dependencies in SAP AVC (Advanced Variant Configuration) models can become quite complex and, thus, confusing. In this case, it’s beneficial to check what’s happening: The trace helps you track which dependencies are executed, when, and with which result. The user’s input and its effects are also visible here.

One caveat upfront: As helpful as the trace is to gain insights into the valuation process, it also impairs performance. So, when you want the dependency machinery to work fast, you better turn off the trace.

This blog post will help you familiarize yourself with the trace feature. After general remarks on its use, we outline an example to illustrate what’s inside the trace log. The important Trace Filters feature is discussed separately, followed by the option to navigate directly to dependencies or objects starting from a trace entry.

 

Use

The trace can be activated in the right pane next to the valuation screen of the simulation UI (the inspector panel). If you don’t see the inspector panel, use the “Toggle Inspector Panel” button at the top right.

 

 

 

Switch from Inspector to Trace and turn on the trace when you’re ready to trace the model’s dependencies.

Use the Search window to find trace entries in large trace logs quickly. The search only works with the technical names of the objects, so you should switch them on in the settings (cogwheel at the top right). The recommendation is to use “Both” (technical name and description).

The following example is supposed to give you an idea of how the trace works and how to read the entries.

 

Example: Check the Gripper

We want to test the model of a robot arm that can pick and drop things. Depending on the environment (“controlled” or “harsh”) and the objects to handle (characteristic “Delicate Parts Handling” can have values “Yes” or “No”), a suitable gripper has to be selected: The “Gripper Technology” can be “Mechanical Gripper” or “Vacuum Gripper.”

It is also possible to choose no value, which can’t be used for the gripper technology, as it is a mandatory characteristic. This is why “(None)” would result in an incomplete configuration.

Let’s turn on the trace and see what happens when we choose the value “Harsh Environment.” This choice triggers the execution of object dependencies because the values for Gripper Technology and Delicate Parts Handling have been determined (“Mechanical Gripper” and “No”), and the user can’t change them anymore.

The trace has a lot going on—68 entries have appeared, as we can see when scrolling down.

But we’re only interested in the entries starting with no. 8, where the user input is processed (our header material is RBT_VA_BUNDLE).

 

User Input

The details for position no. 9 show that the user assigned the value HARSH (the technical name for “Harsh Environment”) to the characteristic RBT_ENVIRONMENT of header material RBT_VA_BUNDLE.

The cogwheel symbols show us the values before the change (cogwheel with arrow pointing to the left) and after the change (arrow pointing to the right). Note that these symbols are only in place with high-level dependencies (as opposed to low-level dependencies for bill of materials (BOM) and routing explosion)! CTRLD is the technical name for “Controlled Environment.”

 

Constraint Execution, Part 1

In the next step, no. 10, the constraint named RBT_VAB_RESTRICT_GRIP_TECH is executed. The bifurcation symbol with the three connected tiny circles stands for “Object Dependency.” The number in round brackets indicates that the first restriction of the constraint is executed. Restrictions are separated by commas so you can see which part is addressed by the number. In our case, there are comments that number the restrictions sequentially.

According to the first restriction in the constraint, the value MECH is chosen by the constraint.

 

Constraint Execution, Part 2

Let’s have a closer look at the editor coding of the constraint. Scrolling down, we can see a second restriction:

This restriction determines that if fragile parts are handled (RBT_WP_FRAGILE = ‘YES’), the gripper must be of the vacuum type (VACM). As an implication, if RBT_GRIP_TECH does not have the value VACM, the characteristic RBT_WP_FRAGILE must have the value NO. This is because restrictions in constraints state general rules and enforce them like ‘laws of nature’ (if applicable, a condition part must be fulfilled upfront, which doesn’t exist here). When a restriction is formulated like “if A, then B,” it’s equivalent to the logical contrapositive “if not B, then not A.”

This being said, we expect the constraint to spring into action again: The characteristic RBT_GRIP_TECH for the grip technology has been set to MECH (i.e., not VACM) during the constraint's first execution, and therefore, RBT_WP_FRAGILE should be set to NO.

As you can tell from step 11 of the trace, this is precisely what happens:

The second restriction of the constraint is executed, picking the value NO for RBT_WP_FRAGILE because RBT_GRIP_TECH has been set to MECH.

 

Trace Filters

The Trace Filters option is a handy feature. You can access it with the funnel symbol button beside the search window.

The Trace Filters pop-up shows you five categories to find results in the trace log:

Trace Level

In the Trace Level, you can narrow your search to high-level or low-level configuration or leave it blank to see both. High-level dependencies are interactive dependencies, and low-level dependencies directly affect BOM and routing. Both can occur interleaved in multi-level models, e.g., when a sales order is validated.

Message Type

You can select one or more entries from this drop-down list. Several entries are connected by an OR logic, which means, e.g., the selection “Values Assignment” plus “Value Restriction” finds all entries where a value is assigned to a characteristic or the domain of values is restricted.

Value Assignment By

Values can be assigned by a user or automatically:

Characteristic

Here, you can enter the names of one or more characteristics using the F4 help.

Dependency

Choose one or more dependencies to look for.

 

Direct Navigation

In the tracing pane, you can directly select a material component, a characteristic, or a dependency. Clicking on an element gives you several options, such as filtering for it or inspecting it.

Let’s click on characteristic RBT_GRIP_TECH in trace entry no. 10, and then choose “Where this was changed”:

As a result, we get back entries no. 10 (again) and no. 61:

This possibility leads us to a best practice in case of an inconsistency: If an inconsistency occurs, search for the “Inconsistency Detected” message type. Then, take the characteristic that has become inconsistent and search upwards using “Where this was changed.”

entry.title: Service with Advanced Execution: Process Flow 4 – Service Quotation with Catalogue Price
entry.content:
Service with Advanced Execution is a new service business process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. This is based on the integration of Service with Maintenance Management.

The concept of Execution Status in Service with Advanced Execution is introduced in the following blog:

Execution Status in Service with Advanced Execution

Within Service with Advanced Execution, there are various process flows depending on the business scenario. These process flows are distinguished based on two aspects:

  • How the process starts, and which is the first transaction.
  • What is the trigger for the creation of the maintenance order and eventually the start of planning.

Process Flow 4:

This blog showcases the process flow wherein the initial transaction is the service order quotation, and the maintenance order is not created in the service order quotation phase.

For example, let us assume that a car dealership offers maintenance and repair services for various car models. A customer requests a standard service, such as an engine oil change or brake replacement, and asks for a quotation. The Service Manager has agreed with the customer to perform fixed price billing. The dealership maintains a price master that lists predefined prices for services and parts.

In such scenarios, the service manager will create a quotation to provide a cost estimate for the service requested by the customer. Since the cost of service is agreed upon with the help of existing price master, there is no need of a detailed planning through maintenance order.

Creation of Service Order Quotation

A service order quotation can be created using the ‘Create Service Quotation’ App.

The following details could be filled while creating a service order quotation document:

  • Sold-to-Party
  • Organizational data such as Sales Organization and optionally Service Organization
  • Parties involved such as Service Employee Group and Employee Responsible
  • Execution Order Item with Planning Plant, Work Center, DIP Profile and Billing Relevance
  • Reference Objects

After entering all the details, save the service order quotation document. The service order quotation gets created with an execution order item with lifecycle status as 'Open'.

Status lifecycle in Service with Advanced Execution

                                                           Current state of an execution order item

The state diagram illustrates the status lifecycle of one execution order item along with its corresponding maintenance order (if any) in this process flow. Each block below represents a state displaying 3 different status values. The lifecycle and execution status of an execution order item along with the status of the corresponding maintenance order.

 

Send to Customer

Once the cost estimate is derived for the requested service, either based on the price master or based on any manual intervention, the quotation can be sent to the customer for approval. When the service order quotation is sent to customer, the execution order item status is updated to 'Released'.

Accept and creation of Follow-up Service Order

When the customer accepts the service order quotation, its lifecycle is updated to 'Completed'. A follow-up service order is automatically created, carrying over the existing execution order item.

Further processing of the service order follows the regular process flow.

Summary

The distinct feature in this process flow is the cost estimation without a maintenance order in a service order quotation phase.

 

Further references

To know more about the execution order item and its attributes, please refer following blog:

Deep Dive: Execution Order Item and Billable Maintenance Order

entry.title: Convert Manually Created Append Fields to Real Key User Custom Fields
entry.content:
Convert Manually Created Append Fields to Real Key User Custom Fields

Have you ever wondered how to convert your manually created "old" append fields into real key user custom fields? Doing so lets you easily expose these fields to OData services, SOAP services, SAP GUI transactions, and more, utilizing the same feature set. Have you ever wondered how to do that without losing your data and preserving your old field name, so that you don’t have to adapt your extension coding which consumes this field?

If your answer is yes, then this blog post is just for you.

Please note that this blog does exclusively address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise and doesn’t address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
In this blog we use S/4HANA as an abbreviation for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise. In addition, for key user custom fields we use the abbreviation custom fields.

This blog post covers the following topics:

  • In which cases you can convert your manually created append fields and
  • How to convert your manually created append fields to custom fields

Custom field technology supports the conversion of manually created append fields to custom fields using transaction SCFD_EUI. These types of custom fields are called ABAP Managed Fields (abbreviation AMF).
This is especially helpful in the following two cases:

  1. You already have a manually created append in the application database table. Due to the high migration effort needed otherwise, you need to keep this field. However, you also have the requirement to bring this field into Fiori UIs, APIs, or analytical applications using the Custom Fields Fiori app.
  2. You have specific requirements regarding the data element of your custom field, which is not supported when using the Custom Fields Fiori app:
    1. Either you need a specific conversion exit on that field or
    2. You need to use a specific check table, which is then used in SAP GUI transactions, for example

Please note that whenever possible, you should use standard custom fields instead of ABAP Managed Fields. Using ABAP Managed Fields significantly increases complexity, especially in software logistics. We will dive into the details of this issue later in this blog post.

How do you enable an append field for the Custom Fields app?

Let’s assume you have an old append field ZZVOUCHER_CODE at hand, which has been added to the extension include structure SDSALESDOC_INCL_EEW_PS. This structure is included in database table VBAK.
You need to preserve the data of this field because the effort of migrating data and custom code to a new custom field with a different field name is too high.
First, make sure that you are in your key user development system and client. In this client, the Adaptation Transport Organizer must be set up. Second, check that you’re using the correct apps for transport handling (also refer to note 2660797) to assure a correct setup for custom field creation. Further details regarding the setup are addressed in the blog post Key User Custom Fields in S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise: Dos and Don’ts.

If everything is ready, open transaction SCFD_EUI. You will see the following selection screen:

Transaction SCFD_EUI: selection screen

In this selection screen, you can filter for database table and field name. An example: enter “VBAK” as database table name and “ZZ*” as database field name. If there are too many fields, you could also enter the entire field name ZZVOUCHER_CODE.
Please note that you get built-in documentation describing the details of transaction SCFD_EUI by clicking the Information button on the top of the transaction.

Transaction SCFD_EUI: resulting list of fields

Now, you see the list of fields that apply to the selection criteria, including your field ZZVOUCHER_CODE.
When you select ZZVOUCHER_CODE, a details section below the field list displays additional information for the selected field. Looking at the Field Status column, you see that the content is different for the listed fields. Whereas the status for field ZZVOUCHER_CODE is Can be Enabled, there are also other values possible. All of these are documented and explained here: Enabling Custom Database Fields for Usage in SAP Fiori Applications.

The next step is to click Enable in the button bar on the top. Now, the pop-up Enable Fields for Use in App Custom Fields and Logic is displayed:

Transaction SCFD_EUI: Enabling an append field

The pop-up allows you to specify the Custom Field ID and other semantics of the custom field (in the screenshot, the value help for the Semantic Type has been selected). Since this needs to be a new custom field and not link to an existing one, we leave the default Enabling Type unchanged.
Note that in transaction SCFD_EUI you can’t influence the prefix of the Custom Field ID, as this must be the customer namespace you configured in the Adaptation Transport Organizer settings. This setting is done via the Prefix field in transaction S_ATO_SETUP.

The field ZZVOUCHER_CODE is character-like and should have a value help assigned. Therefore, we need to change the Semantic Type from the default Text to Code List. Please note that possible semantic types depend on the technical type. In this case, it could also be Email Address, Phone Number or Web Address.

As soon as you select Code List as semantic type, the Value Help View field becomes editable. You can either enter the name of an already existing CDS view or enter the name of new CDS view and select Generate right next to the Value Help View field. Clicking Generate will show a pop-up containing templates for both the CDS Code view and the CDS Text view. You can create the two respective views using ABAP development tools for Eclipse (hereafter abbreviated as ADT), copy the templates, and finally, replace the contained placeholders.

Note that both CDS Code view and  CDS Text view must strictly have the format given within the templates. Also, the CDS Text view must be language-specific. However, the template includes a comment with instructions for alternative actions.

In our case, both CDS views have been prepared in advance: the CDS view ZZVOUCHER_CODE_V as code view and the CDS view ZZVOUCHER_CODE_T as text view. The final data source of these CDS views is the manually created database table ZZD_VOUCHER_CODE. All three artifacts are shown below:

CDS Code View ZZVOUCHER_CODE_V:

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Voucher Code'
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZZVOUCHER_CV'
@search.searchable
@ObjectModel.compositionRoot: true
@ObjectModel.usageType.serviceQuality: #A
@ObjectModel.usageType.sizeCategory: #S
